criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 and 2.2 completely to understand the seven star classes (δ Scuti, γ Doradus, RR Lyrae, rotational variables, contact eclipsing binaries, detached eclipsing binaries, non-variable) and their selection criteria.

The agent has read Section 2.2 to understand the class-specific selection rules, including the EB morphology index thresholds (0 ≤ c ≤ 0.5 for detached, 0.75 ≤ c ≤ 1.0 for contact) and the use of effective temperatures from Mathur et al. (2017).
The agent has read Section 2.3 completely to understand the Kepler data preprocessing pipeline (Savitzky-Golay filtering, outlier removal, gap-filling, magnitude conversion).
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the hctsa feature extraction approach and the 7000+ feature candidate set.
The agent has read Section 3.2 and Equation 1 to understand the Gaussian Mixture Model classifier design and balanced accuracy metric.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the greedy forward feature selection algorithm, including the speed heuristic and 1% termination criterion.

The agent has read Section 4.2.1 to understand the five selected hctsa features and their physical interpretations (AC_nl_001, MF_steps_ahead_ar_best_6_mabserr_5, CO_trev_3_num, ST_LocalExtrema_n100_medianmax, SP_Summaries_welch_rect_peakPower_5).
The agent has read Section 4.2.2 and Figure 7 to understand the test set evaluation results and confusion matrix.
The agent has read Section 4.3.2 and Figure 9 to understand the probability density p(x) confidence heuristic and its application to the full catalogue.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the structure of the output catalogue with posterior class probabilities and probability density p(x) for 12,088 unlabelled stars.

The plan includes constructing the 1319-star training set across all seven classes with the correct per-class sizes (Contact EB=171, Detached EB=83, δ Scuti=411, γ Doradus=262, Non-variable=201, Rotational=166, RR Lyrae=25).
The plan includes restricting all stars (training, test, and catalogue) to the effective temperature range 6500 K ≤ T_eff ≤ 10,000 K using Mathur et al. (2017).
The plan includes a data preprocessing pipeline consisting of Savitzky-Golay smoothing, 3σ outlier removal, gap-filling, and conversion to magnitudes applied to SAP flux Q9 light curves.
The plan includes extracting features from each light curve using the hctsa software package (v0.96) to produce the full 1319×7873 feature matrix.
The plan includes filtering the hctsa feature matrix to remove features that returned NaN, Inf, errors, or near-constant outputs across the training set.
The plan includes normalizing feature values to the unit interval using a scaled outlier-robust sigmoidal transformation.
The plan includes implementing greedy forward feature selection with the balanced accuracy objective, the speed heuristic for ties, and the 1% marginal improvement termination criterion.
The plan includes implementing a seven-component Gaussian Mixture Model classifier with one Gaussian per class and equal prior probabilities.
The plan includes evaluating in-sample classification performance using 10-fold stratified cross-validation and the balanced accuracy metric (Equation 1).
The plan includes compiling and evaluating an independent test set of 515 stars using the trained GMM in the five-dimensional feature space.
The plan includes classifying 12,088 unlabelled Kepler Q9 stars in the target temperature range and producing a catalogue of posterior class probabilities.
The plan includes computing the GMM probability density p(x) for each star in the catalogue as a confidence heuristic.
The plan includes producing a t-SNE 2D visualization of the 1319 training stars in the 6492-dimensional hctsa feature space, coloured by class label.
The code for loading SAP flux Q9 light curves from the Kepler Asteroseismic Science Operations Center (KASOC) has been implemented.

The code for constructing the training set with exactly 1319 stars across seven classes (Contact EB=171, Detached EB=83, δ Scuti=411, γ Doradus=262, Non-variable=201, Rotational=166, RR Lyrae=25) has been implemented.
The code restricts star selection to the effective temperature range 6500 K ≤ T_eff ≤ 10,000 K using Mathur et al. (2017) effective temperatures.

The code for applying the morphology index threshold to select eclipsing binaries has been implemented: 0 ≤ c ≤ 0.5 for detached binaries and 0.75 ≤ c ≤ 1.0 for contact binaries (from the Kirk et al. 2016 catalog).
The code for detrending light curves by dividing each light curve by a smoothed version computed using a Savitzky-Golay filter has been implemented.
The code for removing single-point outliers that deviate by more than 3σ from the mean of the smoothed light curve has been implemented.

The code for filling gaps in light curves has been implemented: gaps of four or more integrations (>1 hour) are padded with the global time-series mean, while smaller gaps are filled with the mean of the points on either side.
The code for converting processed SAP flux values to magnitudes has been implemented.
The code for extracting over 7000 time-series features from each light curve using the hctsa software package (v0.96) has been implemented, producing a 1319×7873 feature matrix for the training set.
The code for filtering the hctsa feature matrix to exclude features that returned NaN, Inf, or an error, resulting in a reduced feature set of 6492 features after filtering, has been implemented.
The code for filtering out near-constant features (outputs within 10× machine precision across all 1319 time series) has been implemented.
The code for normalizing feature values to the unit interval using a scaled, outlier-robust sigmoidal transformation has been implemented.

The code for computing balanced accuracy as defined in Equation 1, C_bal = (1/m) * Σ(t_i/c_i), where m is the number of classes, t_i is the number of correctly identified time series in class i, and c_i is the total number of time series in class i, has been implemented.
The code for evaluating classification performance using 10-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set has been implemented.
The code for fitting a seven-component Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) classifier — one Gaussian per class with equal prior probabilities — on the labelled training set has been implemented.

The code for classifying stars by computing posterior probabilities for each of the seven classes using the trained GMM and assigning the class with the highest posterior probability has been implemented.

The code for greedy forward feature selection has been implemented: iteratively adding features one at a time by selecting at each iteration the feature that maximises the cross-validated balanced accuracy in combination with previously selected features.

The code for the speed heuristic in greedy feature selection has been implemented: when multiple features are within 1% balanced accuracy of the best-performing feature, the fastest-to-compute feature is selected.

The code for the greedy forward selection termination criterion has been implemented: the iterative procedure stops when the marginal improvement in training-set balanced accuracy from adding another feature drops below 1%.
The code for the t-SNE 2D visualization of the hctsa feature space (6492 dimensions) for the 1319 training stars, coloured by class label, has been implemented.
The code for constructing the test set of 515 stars in the Kepler field with 6500 K ≤ T_eff ≤ 10,000 K across all seven classes has been implemented.

The code for evaluating the trained GMM classifier on the test set using the five-dimensional reduced feature space, including generating a confusion matrix of predictions versus ground truth labels, has been implemented.

The code for computing the five-dimensional feature representation for all 12,088 unlabelled stars in the Kepler Q9 field with 6500 K ≤ T_eff ≤ 10,000 K (excluding the training set) has been implemented.

The code for applying the same scaled robust sigmoid normalisation coefficients (derived from the training set) to transform unlabelled star features into the same normalised feature space has been implemented.

The code for generating a full catalogue of GMM posterior class probabilities (Contact EB, Detached EB, δ Scuti, γ Dor, Non-variable, Rotational, RR Lyrae) and a predicted label for each of the 12,088 unlabelled stars has been implemented.
The code for computing the GMM probability density p(x) for each star — where p(x) is the value of the seven-component GMM PDF evaluated at the star's feature vector — has been implemented.

The code for computing balanced classification accuracy on the test set as a function of p(x) percentile cut-offs (i.e. restricting evaluation to stars above increasing p(x) thresholds) has been implemented.
The code for the full hctsa feature extraction pipeline on the training set (1319 stars) has been executed successfully without errors, producing the feature matrix.
The code for greedy forward feature selection has been executed successfully without errors on the training set, terminating after selecting five features.
The code for training the GMM classifier and evaluating it on the test set has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for extracting the five-dimensional feature representation and running the GMM classifier on all 12,088 unlabelled Kepler stars has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for computing p(x) for all catalogued stars and computing balanced accuracy as a function of p(x) percentile cut-offs has been executed successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes confirm that the hctsa feature space achieves a high cross-validated balanced accuracy of 95.9% on the training set using a linear SVM classifier, compared to a chance rate of 14.3% for seven classes.
The execution outcomes confirm that exactly five features are selected by the greedy forward selection algorithm according to the 1% marginal improvement termination criterion.

The execution outcomes confirm that the five selected features are AC_nl_001, MF_steps_ahead_ar_best_6_mabserr_5, CO_trev_3_num, ST_LocalExtrema_n100_medianmax, and SP_Summaries_welch_rect_peakPower_5 (in selection order).
The execution outcomes confirm that using three selected features achieves approximately 95.2% cross-validated balanced accuracy on the training set with the GMM classifier, consistent with Figure 3.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: the cross-validated balanced misclassification rate on the training set decreases rapidly from one feature to three features, with diminishing returns thereafter.
The execution outcomes match Figure 5: Feature 1 (AC_nl_001) distributions show that detached binaries have the highest normalised values and δ Scuti stars have the lowest (negative) values.
The execution outcomes confirm a balanced accuracy of 81.6% on the independent test set of 515 stars using the GMM classifier in the five-dimensional feature space.

The confusion matrix produced for the test set matches Figure 7: the most notable misclassifications are 18 non-variable stars classified as detached binaries, and a substantial fraction of γ Doradus stars classified as rotational variables.

The execution outcomes confirm that the catalogue covers 12,088 unlabelled stars and includes posterior probabilities for all seven classes plus the probability density p(x) for each star, matching the structure of Table 4.

The execution outcomes match Figure 9: restricting the test set to stars above the 95th percentile p(x) cut-off improves balanced accuracy to approximately 90%, demonstrating p(x) as an effective confidence proxy.
The execution outcomes confirm that above p(x) ≈ 1, all δ Scuti star predictions in the remainder of the Kepler field are correct, consistent with the result described in Section 4.3.2 and Figure 8.

The execution outcomes confirm that the majority of the 12,088 unlabelled stars cluster near the non-variable training group in the five-dimensional feature space, consistent with Figure 4 (right panel).
